title: Lesson 335
transcript: Last video on cryptographic attacks. There's just a few more that we need to talk about here. First one, birthday attacks. So we actually, John actually talked about a birthday attack in relation to hashing. And if you recall, the whole purpose of the birthday attack is to try and detect collisions. The whole reason that we're trying to look for collisions is we want to try and defeat hashing, essentially. And so there's basically four terms that you should just associate in your mind. So how do we achieve integrity? With hashing. How do we attack hashing algorithms? By trying to find collisions. How can we try to efficiently find collisions? with the birthday attack. So just remember those, birthday attack, collisions, hashing, integrity. Integrity, hashing, collisions, birthday attack. Just remember those four terms together and that will help you answer a lot of questions, right? The way that we achieve integrity is with hashing. The way that we try and attack hashing is by finding collisions. An efficient method of finding collisions is the birthday attack. Now, Kerberos. Kerberos is a single sign-on protocol that we're going to talk about in a lot more detail in Domain 5. Kerberos has been around for decades. Version 4 of Kerberos was ratified in the 80s. The most recent version of Kerberos, version 5, was ratified in the 90s. And that's the most recent version. Kerberos is a very old protocol. And it has a lot of issues. And... Despite that, despite the fact that it has quite a few issues, it's still a pervasively used protocol. And as we'll talk about in Domain 5, the reason it's so pervasively used is just because it's built into everything. It's become essentially the default standard for single sign-on. And so I'm going to just cover it a little bit here, and then we'll talk about it more in Domain 5. But there's a couple of major issues with Kerberos. The first that you need to know about is that Kerberos version 4 only supports DES encryption. And as John talked about just a little while ago, DES only uses a 56-bit key. And is a 56-bit key considered to be good enough nowadays? Absolutely not. You can pretty easily, especially organizations like the NSA, they can probably brute force a DES key in seconds. And so version 4 of Kerberos only supports DES, which is not considered to be secure nowadays. Version 5 of Kerberos has a nice upgrade to Triple DES and IDEA, and those are both considered to be secure algorithms nowadays, right? Anything greater than 80 bits is reasonably secure, and Triple DES, of course, is well above that, and so is IDEA. So that's great. It uses secure algorithms, but they're symmetric. algorithms. And one of the major issues you need to deal with in a large Kerberos realm is you're going to have a lot of users, you're going to have a lot of services. There's a ton of encryption keys that you have to exchange. And of course, what's the Achilles heel of symmetric cryptography? key exchange. And so there's a real challenge with securely exchanging all these keys with Kerberos, all these symmetric keys with Kerberos version 4. So that's kind of the major high-level issues you should know about Kerberos. Final thing we're going to talk about here in the cryptographic attacks are social engineering cryptographic attacks. So this is where obviously the attacker uses various social engineering techniques to try and break encryption algorithms or encryption systems. And by the way, we did not make up the names of these. These are the industry standard names. that you need to know for the exam. The first type of social engineering attack that you need to know about is the purchase key attack. And this is exactly what it sounds like. If you want to figure out the, say, the encryption key for a system, just bribe someone, right? Give them a suitcase full of money and... Bribe them. That's a purchase key attack. And it's at this point that I'd like to highlight something very important. John and I are very, and Lou as well, we're very susceptible to a purchase key attack as it relates to the exam answers. So, you know, drop us an email. Let me know what that would be worth to you. Now. That's purchase key attack. Rubber hose cryptanalysis. Again, didn't make this name up. Rubber hose cryptanalysis is where you torture someone to figure out some cryptographic information. So it's at this point that I'd like to highlight something even more important. We are not susceptible to rubber hose cryptanalysis as it relates to the exam answers. We don't actually know the exam answers. So please do not try and figure out what we live and show up in our door and try and torture us. We don't actually know the answers. So that's rubber hose cryptanalysis. You torture someone. And on that really, really weird note, you've reached the very end of cryptography. And here's, of course, a great XKCD comic to finish off this section with. Nice example here of rubber hose cryptanalysis. So well done you. You have made it through everything related to cryptography. It was a bit of a journey, wasn't it? But hopefully you've learned a lot. And hopefully you now know what you need to know. Not hopefully. You now know what you need to know from a cryptography perspective for the CISSP exam. ðŸŽµ
